name: Serial Killers
description: Notorious serial killers from true-crime history, plus the terminology used to categorize them.

questions.q: What single moniker was given to the never-identified killer who terrorized Whitechapel in 1888?

source.notes: Attributed to five canonical Whitechapel murders, 1888.

questions.q: Which American serial killer, active in the 1970s, was nicknamed 'the Killer Clown'?

source.notes: Performed as 'Pogo the Clown' at parties.

questions.q: Which Milwaukee, Wisconsin, killer was convicted in 1992 of murdering 17 men and boys?

source.notes: Murdered between 1978 and 1991.

questions.q: Which infamous killer lured victims by feigning an injury, such as a broken arm in a sling?

source.notes: Confessed to 30 murders across seven U.S. states.

questions.q: Which self-styled 'Son of Sam' claimed his neighbor's dog told him to kill?

source.notes: Claimed the dog named Harvey gave instructions.

questions.q: The cult 1991 film 'The Silence of the Lambs' features a fictional serial killer who skins his victims. What is his nickname?

source.notes: Character Buffalo Bill was inspired by Gein and others.

questions.q: Which northern California killer signed taunting letters with a crosshair-style symbol and was never caught?

source.notes: Active in the San Francisco Bay Area in the late 1960s; ciphers mailed to newspapers.

questions.q: The FBI classifies someone as a serial murderer after how many separate killings?

source.notes: Per FBI, two or more victims in separate events, per the 2005 Symposium definition.

questions.q: FBI profiler Robert Ressler is credited with coining which enduring phrase in the 1970s?

source.notes: Often credited with popularizing the term in U.S. law enforcement.

questions.q: Which English doctor, dubbed 'Dr. Death,' murdered as many as 250 patients before being caught in 1998?

source.notes: Manchester GP, convicted 2000.

questions.q: What was the grim nickname of Dennis Rader, who terrorized Kansas from the 1970s to the early 1990s?

source.notes: Wichita-area; arrested 2005 after taunting letters resumed.

questions.q: Which Chicago con man ran a so-called 'Murder Castle' during the 1893 World's Fair?

source.notes: Subject of Erik Larson's 'The Devil in the White City.'

questions.q: Which Colombian serial killer, known as 'La Bestia,' confessed to murdering more than 130 boys?

source.notes: Sentenced in 1999 for 138 confirmed killings.

questions.q: Which 16th-century Hungarian countess was said to have bathed in the blood of young women?

source.notes: Legend exaggerated, but she was convicted of murdering servant girls.

questions.q: Ed Gein, whose crimes inspired films like Psycho and The Texas Chain Saw Massacre, operated in which U.S. state?

source.notes: Plainfield, Wisconsin — discovered 1957.

questions.q: Which Russian killer, dubbed the 'Rostov Ripper,' murdered over 50 people from 1978 to 1990?

source.notes: Convicted of 52 murders.

questions.q: The Green River Killer terrorized which U.S. region during the 1980s and 1990s?

source.notes: Ridgway, Washington State; pleaded guilty to 48 murders.

questions.q: Which Los Angeles killer of the mid-1980s was nicknamed 'The Night Stalker'?

source.notes: Convicted 1989; 13 murders.

questions.q: Which FBI computer system, built to link unsolved violent crimes nationwide, was championed by retired LAPD detective Pierce Brooks?

source.notes: Violent Criminal Apprehension Program, launched 1985.

questions.q: Which 15th-century French nobleman and Joan of Arc companion was later executed for murdering children?

source.notes: Sometimes cited as the basis of the Bluebeard legend.

questions.q: Which German killer of the 1920s was known as the 'Vampire of Düsseldorf'?

source.notes: Executed 1931. Haarmann was the 'Butcher of Hanover' — different man.

questions.q: The early-20th-century Frenchman Henri Landru, who wooed and murdered ten women during WWI, was nicknamed after which fairy-tale character?

source.notes: Landru — the 'Bluebeard of Gambais,' guillotined 1922.
